title: Log-concavity, matroids and expanders
speaker: Cynthia Vinzant
affiliation: North Carolina State University; von Neumann Fellow, School of Mathematics
abstract: Matroids are combinatorial objects that model various types of independence. They appear several fields mathematics, including graph theory, combinatorial optimization, and algebraic geometry. In this talk, I will introduce the theory of matroids along with the closely related class of polynomials called strongly log-concave polynomials. Strong log-concavity is a functional property of a real multivariate polynomial that translates to useful conditions on its coefficients. Closed related classes are real stable and Lorentzian polynomials.

proof_stages.label: Generating-polynomial dictionary for finite measures
proof_stages.summary: The talk begins by associating a nonnegative multivariate generating polynomial to a finite probability distribution and explaining that polynomial operations such as differentiation correspond to probabilistic operations such as conditioning.

proof_stages.label: Strong log-concavity and stable-polynomial sources
proof_stages.summary: Strong log-concavity is defined as log-concavity of a polynomial and all its derivatives on the positive orthant, with stable polynomials presented as a major prior class that automatically satisfies it and carries stronger properties such as negative dependence.

proof_stages.label: Matroids as the supports of squarefree strongly log-concave polynomials
proof_stages.summary: After reviewing the matroid exchange axiom and standard independence models, the speaker states the central support theorem: homogeneous squarefree strongly log-concave polynomials have matroidal support, and every matroid basis-generating polynomial is strongly log-concave.

proof_stages.label: Hessian signature mechanism for log-concavity
proof_stages.summary: Log-concavity is translated into a Hessian inequality showing that the ordinary Hessian has at most one positive eigenvalue, and a convex-combination lemma explains how such local signature conditions can be propagated.

proof_stages.label: Graphical/Lorentzian test for strong log-concavity
proof_stages.summary: To each derivative of a homogeneous polynomial the speaker associates a Hessian graph, then gives the finite criterion that strong log-concavity is equivalent to connectedness of these derivative graphs together with the one-positive-eigenvalue condition at the quadratic level, identifying this with the Lorentzian-polynomial viewpoint.

proof_stages.label: Markov-chain and high-dimensional-expander consequences
proof_stages.summary: Normalizing Hessian-type matrices produces Markov chains on faces of weighted simplicial complexes, and the local spectral criteria from high-dimensional expander theory are shown to coincide with strong log-concavity and to imply rapid mixing for walks such as those on matroid bases.

proof_stages.label: Summary and open extension program
proof_stages.summary: The conclusion packages strongly log-concave polynomials as a class with good examples, closure operations, combinatorial consequences, and sampling algorithms, and lists open directions such as preservation operations, convex programming analogues, and extensions beyond matroids.

examples.name: Determinantal distribution from squared minors
examples.note: A finite distribution on d-subsets is built from squared d-by-d minors of a short fat matrix, illustrating how a probability measure becomes a nonnegative generating polynomial favoring well-spread vector sets.

examples.name: Rank-two uniform matroid on four elements
examples.note: Four generic vectors in R^2 give the matroid with all two-subsets as bases, later reappearing as the elementary symmetric polynomial whose Hessian, associated graph, and small Markov chain illustrate the spectral criteria.
examples.engagement_reason: he uses the rank-two uniform matroid on four elements to compute the basis polynomial Hessian, its eigenvalue signature, and a concrete random-walk transition rule
